title: Acyclicity
tex_block_html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is called acyclic if there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself in $G$ for any node $v \in G$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the walk definitions of def def:walks. By a directed walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ (for $v, w \in J \cup V$) we mean a directed walk in the sense of def def:walks, item ii., admitted to be of any length $n \ge 0$: a tuple $(v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ with $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$, $v_0 = v$, $v_n = w$, and $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E$ for every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$ (in the LN's diagrammatic shorthand of def not-cdmg, item 2: $v = v_0 \tuh v_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_{n-1} \tuh v_n = w$). Such a directed walk is called non-trivial iff its length $n$ satisfies $n \ge 1$, equivalently iff it traverses at least one edge; the length-$0$ trivial directed walk $(v_0)$ at $v_0 = v = w$ (admitted by def def:walks, item ii., as the single-vertex tuple with no edges) is excluded.

The CDMG $G$ is called acyclic iff \[ \text{for every } v \in J \cup V, \quad \text{there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from } v \text{ to itself in } G. \] Spelled out as a first-order statement over the components $J, V, E$ of $G$: for every $v \in J \cup V$, every integer $n \ge 1$, and every tuple $(v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n) \in (J \cup V)^{n+1}$ with $v_0 = v_n = v$, there exists some $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$ with $(v_k, v_{k+1}) \notin E$ (i.e.\ no such vertex tuple is the vertex sequence of a directed walk from $v$ to itself in $G$).

Consequence: no directed self-loops on output nodes. For any $v \in V$, a directed self-loop on $v$ – i.e.\ the ordered pair $(v, v) \in E$, equivalently $v \tuh v \in G$ in the notation of def not-cdmg, item 2 – yields the length-$1$ directed walk $\bigl(v, (v, v), v\bigr)$ from $v$ to itself, which is non-trivial in the sense above. Hence if $G$ is acyclic, then $(v, v) \notin E$ for every $v \in V$. (For $v \in J$, the CDMG-type constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ together with $J \cap V = \emptyset$ already excludes $(v, v) \in E$ a priori, so the acyclicity hypothesis adds no further constraint at $v \in J$.) In particular, an acyclic CDMG contains no directed self-loops on any $v \in V$.

lean_blocks.explanation: Lean 4's `variable` command does not produce a declaration of its own; instead it tells the elaborator that any later `def`, `theorem`, or `lemma` in this scope that mentions `Node` should have these binders auto-inserted at the front of its signature. The braces around `{Node : Type*}` make `Node` an *implicit* argument inferred from later arguments (e.g. from `G : CDMG Node`), and `Type*` is Mathlib shorthand for a universe-polymorphic `Type u`. The `[DecidableEq Node]` is an instance argument supplying decidable equality on the carrier. Both fixtures are load-bearing for the `IsAcyclic` definition below: `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` (from `def_3_4`) recurses through the walk and on each step checks `a ∈ G.E`, a `Finset (Node × Node)` membership that needs decidable equality on pairs of `Node`. The same instance also drives the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2`, which unfolds `v ∈ G` to `Finset.mem` on `G.J ∪ G.V`. Drop either fixture and the next block fails to type-check.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-implicits for every later decl: load-bearing for `v ∈ G` and `Walk.IsDirectedWalk`'s per-edge `Finset` checks.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: Lean 4's `variable` command does not produce a declaration of its own; instead it tells the elaborator that any later `def`, `theorem`, or `lemma` in this scope that mentions `Node` should have these binders auto-inserted at the front of its signature. The braces around `{Node : Type*}` make `Node` an *implicit* argument inferred from later arguments (e.g. from `G : CDMG Node`), and `Type*` is Mathlib shorthand for a universe-polymorphic `Type u`. The `[DecidableEq Node]` is an instance argument supplying decidable equality on the carrier. Both fixtures are load-bearing for the `IsAcyclic` definition below: `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` (from `def_3_4`) recurses through the walk and on each step checks `a ∈ G.E`, a `Finset (Node × Node)` membership that needs decidable equality on pairs of `Node`. The same instance also drives the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2`, which unfolds `v ∈ G` to `Finset.mem` on `G.J ∪ G.V`. Drop either fixture and the next block fails to type-check.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-implicits for every later decl: load-bearing for `v ∈ G` and `Walk.IsDirectedWalk`'s per-edge `Finset` checks.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
def length : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => 0
  | _, _, .cons _ _ p => p.length + 1

def IsAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) : Prop :=
  ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ ∃ p : Walk G v v, p.IsDirectedWalk ∧ p.length ≥ 1
lean_blocks.explanation: `IsAcyclic G` captures, as a `Prop`, the property that the CDMG `G` admits no non-trivial directed walk from any node back to itself. The quantifier `∀ v ∈ G` is sugar resolved by the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance introduced in `def_3_2`: it unfolds to `∀ v, v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V → …`, so `v` ranges over the LN's `J ∪ V`. The witness type `Walk G v v` is the `def_3_4` walk inductive *indexed on both endpoints*, so "from `v` to itself" is encoded in the type — no extra equality side condition is needed. The two conjuncts under the existential pin down what "non-trivial directed walk" means: `p.IsDirectedWalk` says every edge of `p` lies in `G.E` (the directed-edge `Finset`), and `p.length ≥ 1` excludes the length-$0$ trivial walk `Walk.nil`, requiring at least one `cons` constructor — i.e. at least one edge traversed. In particular, this forbids directed self-loops `(v, v) ∈ G.E` on any output node `v \in G.V`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- `IsAcyclic G : Prop` — predicate on a CDMG `G` saying it has no non-trivial directed self-walk.
def IsAcyclic (G : CDMG Node) : Prop :=
  -- For every node `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, no walk `p : Walk G v v` is both directed and length ≥ 1.
  ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ ∃ p : Walk G v v, p.IsDirectedWalk ∧ p.length ≥ 1

addition_to_the_LN: [nontrivial_directed_walk_not_defined_in_block] A non-trivial directed walk is a directed walk $v_0 \tuh \cdots \tuh v_n$ with $n \ge 1$ (i.e., one that traverses at least one edge); the trivial walk consisting of a single node $v_0$ is excluded. In particular, a self-loop $v \tuh v$ constitutes a non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself, so an acyclic CDMG contains no self-loops on any $v \in V$.
design_choices:
**Typing context `Node : Type*` with `[DecidableEq Node]`, inherited from `def_3_1`.** The decidability instance is load-bearing for this row in two distinct places. First, the `Walk.IsDirectedWalk` predicate from `def_3_4` recurses on per-edge `a ∈ G.E` membership checks, and `G.E` is a `Finset` of pairs of `Node` — `Finset.mem` reduces to decidable pair equality on `Node`. Second, the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2` (in `CDMGNotation.lean`), which drives the `v ∈ G` quantifier below, unfolds to `Finset.mem` on `G.J ∪ G.V`, again routing through `DecidableEq Node`. Dropping the instance would make the statement fail to type-check, while stronger instances (`Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) are not required here and are deferred to consumers that need them.

**Three-dash `--- start helper` marker rather than the two-dash variant.** The two-dash flavour `-- def_3_6 -- start statement` is reserved for declarations whose body is the formalised LN content of the row. The `variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]` line, by contrast, is statement-typing infrastructure — it binds the implicit parameters the `IsAcyclic` definition then references, but it is not itself part of the LN definition. The three-dash flavour signals exactly this distinction to the tex/Lean reconciliation tooling and to the website extractor, both of which read by marker. The convention is shared across `CDMG.lean`, `CDMGNotation.lean`, `Walks.lean`, `EdgeRelations.lean`, and `CDMGRestrictions.lean`.

**Quantifier scope `∀ v ∈ G`, mirroring the LN's literal `v \in J \cup V`.** The `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance unfolds `v ∈ G` to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, so the LN phrasing transports verbatim onto the Lean text with no helper notation or macro. The narrower scope `∀ v ∈ G.V` would be logically equivalent — the `J`-half is vacuous because `def_3_1`'s `hE_subset` forces every directed edge's target into `G.V` and `hJV_disj` keeps `J ∩ V = ∅`, so no length-$\ge 1$ directed walk can start and return to any `j ∈ G.J` — but restricting in that direction would drift away from the LN's literal text and force every downstream rule that destructures `v ∈ G` to carry an extra $J$-versus-$V$ case split.

**"Non-trivial" pinned as `p.length ≥ 1` via the operator's clarification of an LN gap.** The LN block uses "non-trivial" without defining it; the addition `[nontrivial_directed_walk_not_defined_in_block]` pins it to walks of length $n \ge 1$ — at least one edge traversed — and admits a self-loop $v \rightharpoonup v$ as a non-trivial directed walk of length $1$. Two plausible readings were on the table (length $\ge 1$ versus length $\ge 2$; only the latter would let an acyclic CDMG carry directed self-loops), and we resolve to the former. The downstream consequence is the no-self-loop corollary spelled out in the rewritten tex spec: a directed self-loop $(v, v) \in E$ on any $v \in G.V$ yields the length-$1$ walk $\bigl(v, (v, v), v\bigr)$, immediately violating `IsAcyclic`. `Walk.length` (in `Walks.lean`) counts edges via `nil ↦ 0` and `cons ↦ length p + 1`, so `p.length ≥ 1` is exactly "at least one `cons` constructor". Alternative encodings `0 < p.length`, `¬ p.length = 0`, or `p ≠ Walk.nil v hv` are logically equivalent but `p.length ≥ 1` reads most directly off the LN's `$n \ge 1$`.

**`Walk G v v` rather than a fresh `Cycle` or `Path` type.** The upstream `def_3_4` `Walk` inductive indexes on endpoints, so "from $v$ to itself" lives in the *type* `Walk G v v` — no side condition "`p` starts and ends at `v`" needs to be threaded through. A separate `Cycle G v` inductive would force a forgetful `Cycle → Walk` coercion at every downstream site that mixes acyclicity with general-walk reasoning (`def_3_8`'s topological order, `claim_3_2`'s acyclic-iff-topological-order, and the $d$- and $\sigma$-separation arguments of later chapters that case-split on whether a sub-walk is directed), duplicating `Walk`'s structural recursion for no gain. A `Path` type with `Nodup` vertex sequence would actively *exclude* the LN-admitted length-$1$ self-loop case — the wrong shape for this row's predicate.

**Conjunction `p.IsDirectedWalk ∧ p.length ≥ 1` under the existential, not a subtype or a bundled `DirectedCycle` struct.** The LN prose reads "a non-trivial directed walk" — two independent constraints on the same walk `p`. A subtype `{p : Walk G v v // p.IsDirectedWalk ∧ p.length ≥ 1}` would force every consumer of `¬ ∃ p, …` to unpack `Subtype.mk` and `Subtype.property` before reaching the underlying walk; a bundled `DirectedCycle` structure would commit downstream rows to a parallel walk-flavoured notion that does not otherwise appear in the chapter. A plain `∧` keeps inversion ergonomic: a hypothesis `¬ ∃ p, p.IsDirectedWalk ∧ p.length ≥ 1` decomposes by `rintro ⟨p, hp_dir, hp_len⟩` directly — exactly the shape `claim_3_2` will pattern-match against when constructing or rejecting witness cycles, and exactly what `def_3_8`'s topological-order construction needs when ruling out backward edges.

**Mathlib re-use is not on the table.** `SimpleGraph.Walk` is for undirected single-channel graphs and carries no notion of directed-versus-bidirected edges or a $J$/$V$ input/output partition, so it cannot encode a CDMG walk. `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic` then forbids only undirected cycles of length $\ge 3$ — missing both the directed-walk constraint (the LN admits non-directed bidirected walks freely) and the length-$1$ self-loop case the addition pins down. Rolling our own `IsAcyclic` on top of our own `Walk` is the only option that stays close to the LN definition.
